text:
KINGS NEUROSCIENCES CENTRE


Clinic Date: 06 November 2020 

Dr Wang
Saffron Park Hospital

Saffron Park, London, E14 7JL
Dear Dr Wang
John Doe, DOB: 09-02-1941, Hospital No: P546484 NHS No. 5484656746
Flat 15 Roundwood Road, London, E14 7JL 

I reviewed the above patient in the Neurology Clinic today by video link with the ward team. He is a bedbound older adult residing in a nursing facility, and most of the history was provided by his named nurse and the weekday caregiver, who also maintain a seizure observation log.

The caregiving staff report variability in recent events, noting “one or three seizures last month” depending on which entries were counted as full events versus brief episodes without clear impairment. They describe brief behavioural arrest with unresponsiveness, occasional right arm tremor, and post-event confusion lasting up to 20 minutes. There has been no reported tongue biting or head injury. No clear daytime pattern; some episodes occurred around early morning care. The team deny intercurrent infection, but he has had reduced oral intake on some days.

He is currently on Levetiracetam 750 mg b.d. and Lamotrigine 50 mg b.d., administered crushed with soft food. He has tolerated these without rash or clear mood change. The nurse notes intermittent daytime somnolence and mild dizziness on sitting up, which may be multifactorial given his frailty and concurrent medications for blood pressure and constipation. They also report occasional myoclonic jerks during repositioning, which are not clearly time-linked to the recorded seizures.

Examination today was limited by the remote setting. He was rousable to voice, oriented to person, followed one-step commands inconsistently, with no focal asymmetry appreciated on gross limb movements. No cardiorespiratory distress. Skin intact with pressure-relief measures in place. Most observations were relayed by staff.

Plan:
- Continue current antiepileptic regimen unchanged for now given the nursing team’s report of “one or three seizures last month” and the uncertainty about which events represent definite seizures.
- The facility will maintain a structured seizure diary capturing: exact time, witnessed features (including eye deviation, limb involvement, automatisms), duration, recovery phase, possible triggers (missed doses, sleep disruption, infection), and pre-event observations.
- Arrange serum levels for Lamotrigine and routine bloods (U&E, LFT, FBC) through the nursing facility within the next two weeks to check for metabolic contributors and adherence proxies; ensure results are forwarded to the clinic.
- Request an in-reach community EEG if feasible to help characterise events, acknowledging the limitations in a bedbound patient.
- Nursing advice: maintain strict medication administration times, ensure hydration, and continue pressure-area care. Observe for any new focal deficits or prolonged events (>5 minutes), for which emergency protocols should be followed.
- We will review in 8–10 weeks via telemedicine with the same caregivers present and the updated seizure diary to guide any medication adjustments.

Please let me know if there are changes in his baseline cognition, suspected infection, or if the events escalate in frequency or duration before the planned review.

Yours sincerely

entities.text: one or three seizures last month

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter explicitly states the caregiving staff report variability: “one or three seizures last month,” with uncertainty about which events were definite seizures. This provides a numeric range for the most recent month. Per the rules, prioritize explicit numeric expressions and, when multiple frequencies exist, select the highest frequency (more frequent). Therefore, use 3 per month. No other specific frequency period is given, and not seizure-free. Quote the verbatim phrase.
reference: one or three seizures last month
